_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
min_doctor_qualification: MCh/DNB/Equivalent (in Pediatric surgery, CTVS, Surgical Gastroenterology)
clinical_indications: Presence of a diaphragmatic hernia (protrusion of abdominal contents into the thoracic cavity through a defect in the diaphragm) confirmed by imaging (e.g., CT).

Patient presenting with symptoms such as dyspnea on exertion, chest discomfort, decreased air entry, or abdominal complaints (recurrent abdominal pain, postprandial fullness, vomiting, obstructive gastrointestinal symptoms).
Asymptomatic diaphragmatic hernia revealed upon imaging studies, requiring management.
Need for surgical repair as the management, with mesh repair considered if primary closure is not possible.

preauth.label: Clinical notes including evaluation findings, indication of implant requirement (if applicable), and planned line of management

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes contain detailed history, especially history of trauma, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure, and planned line of treatment?
Did the patient present with dyspnea on exertion, chest discomfort/ decreased air entry, abdominal complaints/ asymptomatic?
Did the imaging confirm diaphragmatic hernia?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Was the imaging indicative of surgery?
Did the Post procedure X-ray Chest/Abdomen show repair of the defect?
Are implant invoice/barcode details available if applicable?
Is FIR documentation available if applicable (in case of trauma)?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advice at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Clinical condition or imaging was not indicative of surgery
Clinical notes are incomplete or do not clearly state evaluation findings, implant requirement (if applicable), or planned line of management
Missing detailed operative/procedure notes at claim submission
Missing post-operative X-ray Chest/Abdomen report at claim submission
Missing implant details (invoice/barcode) at claim submission if an implant was used
Missing FIR documentation in cases of trauma at claim submission

clinical_key_pointers: A diaphragmatic hernia (DH) is a protrusion of abdominal contents into the thoracic cavity as a result of a defect within the diaphragm.
Types of Diaphragmatic hernia include Congenital (refer to relevant PMJAY guidance document) and Acquired.
Acquired diaphragmatic hernia (ADH) is rare; diaphragmatic rupture from thoracoabdominal trauma occurs in approximately 0.8 to 3.6% of cases, with relatively low incidence of herniation.

Common etiologies for acquired diaphragmatic hernias in adulthood are traumatic diaphragmatic rupture (penetrating injury 65%, blunt trauma 35%), hiatus hernia (refer to relevant PMJAY guidance document), and iatrogenic causes.

Clinical presentation of ADH can vary, including respiratory (shortness of breath, chest pain), abdominal (recurrent abdominal pain, postprandial fullness, vomiting, obstructive gastrointestinal symptoms), and sometimes cardiac symptoms.
Some spontaneous cases of DH may remain asymptomatic and only revealed upon imaging studies.
Physical examination may reveal absence of breath sounds over the lower chest area, with bowel sounds being audible.
Imaging, especially Computed Tomography (CT), is vital for diagnosis of acquired diaphragmatic hernia.
surgical_options: Management following appropriate resuscitation is surgical repair.
If primary closure is not possible, mesh repair may be an alternative.
Surgical repair may utilize open (abdominal, thoracic, or thoracic-abdominal approach) or laparoscopic approach, depending on the timing of clinical diagnosis.
additional_information.pre_op_preparation: Treatment of acquired diaphragmatic hernia in the acute setting requires appropriate resuscitation of the patient.
additional_information.referral_criteria: Decide referral of cases to the appropriate level of care (as required) for treatment of patients under PMJAY and selection of corresponding Health Benefit Package.
